description: Fix catastrophic regex backtracking (ReDoS) in validateUrlPattern host validation. The current regex /^(\*\.)?[a-z0-9]+([-.]?[a-z0-9]+)*\.[a-z]{2,}$/i has nested quantifiers that cause exponential backtracking on certain inputs. A malformed plugin package.json with a crafted urlPatterns entry can freeze the MCP server during plugin discovery for minutes to hours, making it unresponsive to all MCP clients.

userStories.title: Fix ReDoS in validateUrlPattern host regex
userStories.description: As a platform operator, I want the URL pattern validation regex to be safe from catastrophic backtracking so that a malformed or malicious plugin cannot freeze the MCP server during discovery.
acceptanceCriteria: The host validation regex in validateUrlPattern (platform/shared/src/index.ts) is replaced with an equivalent regex that does not have nested quantifiers competing for the same character class

The replacement regex uses atomic-style grouping or a restructured pattern that prevents backtracking — e.g., /^(\*\.)?[a-z0-9]+([-.][a-z0-9]+)*\.[a-z]{2,}$/i (making the separator [-.]mandatory inside the repeat group, not optional)
All existing unit tests in platform/shared/src/validation.test.ts continue to pass with the new regex
A new test case verifies that a pathological input (e.g., 'a'.repeat(50) + '!') completes validation in under 100ms
userStories.notes:
## The Bug

File: `platform/shared/src/index.ts`, line 427.

The host validation regex is:
```
/^(\*\.)?[a-z0-9]+([-.]?[a-z0-9]+)*\.[a-z]{2,}$/i
```

The problem is `[a-z0-9]+([-.]?[a-z0-9]+)*` — when `[-.]?` matches nothing (i.e., no separator), both `[a-z0-9]+` (the outer required group) and `[a-z0-9]+` (inside the repeat) compete for the same characters. When the overall match fails (no valid `.tld` at the end), the engine tries all possible ways to split the characters between the two groups, causing exponential backtracking.

Empirical proof: 25 alphanumeric chars + '!' = 488ms, 30 chars = 15,642ms. 35 chars would take ~500 seconds.

## The Fix

Make the separator `[-.]` mandatory (not optional) inside the repeat group:
```
/^(\*\.)?[a-z0-9]+([-.][a-z0-9]+)*\.[a-z]{2,}$/i
```

With `[-.]` required (no `?`), the inner group can only match after consuming a separator character. The outer `[a-z0-9]+` greedily consumes all alphanumeric characters up to the first separator, and the inner group only activates after a `-` or `.`. There is no ambiguity about which group owns which characters, so no backtracking occurs.

This change is semantically equivalent for valid hostnames because valid domain labels are separated by dots (already covered by the required `\.` before the TLD) or hyphens. A sequence of pure alphanumeric characters without any separator is just one label — handled entirely by the outer `[a-z0-9]+`.

## Callers

This function is called from:
- `platform/mcp-server/src/loader.ts:417` — during plugin discovery (freezes the MCP server)
- `platform/plugin-tools/src/commands/build.ts` — during plugin build
- `platform/create-plugin/src/index.ts` — during plugin scaffolding

## Test

Add a test case in `platform/shared/src/validation.test.ts` in the existing `validateUrlPattern` describe block:
```typescript
it('completes in bounded time for pathological input (ReDoS protection)', () => {
  const start = Date.now();
  const result = validateUrlPattern('*://' + 'a'.repeat(50) + '!/path');
  const elapsed = Date.now() - start;
  expect(result).not.toBeNull(); // invalid host — returns error string
  expect(elapsed).toBeLessThan(100); // must complete in under 100ms
});
```
